summary: DeVault walks through the architecture of mrsh, a strictly POSIX-compliant shell implementation by Simon Ser. The shell has two main components: a recursive descent parser that produces an AST, and a task-based execution system. The parser deliberately avoids dependencies like flex/bison in favor of simplicity. The AST is exposed as a public library interface for use in editors, linters, and syntax highlighters. Execution converts AST nodes into pollable tasks that can wait on subprocesses or defer to sub-tasks. The post also covers POSIX built-in commands and why certain commands must be implemented within the shell itself.
key_points: mrsh uses a recursive descent parser to produce an AST whose node names map directly to the POSIX shell specification
The parser avoids flex/bison dependencies in favor of simpler, self-contained code since shell parsing performance isn't critical
The AST is exposed as a public library (libmrsh) enabling reuse in editors, linters, and syntax highlighters
Execution uses a poll-based task interface that can return immediately with wait/error/done status, avoiding blocking
POSIX mandates certain built-in commands (like cd and .) because they must manipulate shell state directly
money_quotes: There are two main components to a shell: parsing and execution.
This design was chosen to simplify the code and avoid dependencies like flex/bison, and is a good choice given that performance isn't critical for parsing shell scripts.
Most of these names come directly from the POSIX shell specification.
The parser and AST is made available as a standalone public interface of libmrsh, which can be used for a variety of use-cases like syntax-aware text editors, syntax highlighting, linters, etc.
Most of these AST nodes becomes a task.
Some commands have to be built-in because they manipulate the shell's state, such as . and cd.
key_insight: A well-designed POSIX shell cleanly separates parsing into a reusable AST library from a poll-based task execution system, keeping the implementation simple and composable.
